posts.title: SDRs make the best GTM engineers
posts.hook: I sent 200+ cold emails a day from primary domains with no warmup on SalesLoft. No sequences. No AI. Just me and a Salesforce account I barely understood.
posts.body:
I sent 200+ cold emails a day from primary domains with no warmup on SalesLoft. No sequences. No AI. Just me and a Salesforce account I barely understood.

Now I build the systems that automate what I used to do by hand.

The gap between those two versions of me isn't "learning to code." It's pattern recognition. When you've manually built buying committees in Salesforce, you know exactly which fields matter. When you've been burned by bad data, you know what enrichment actually needs to check.

Every Clay workflow I build starts with a question I asked myself at 7am in 2019 staring at a spreadsheet. Every scoring rubric maps back to signals I learned to read from sending thousands of emails and watching what landed.

The AI agent discourse right now is all infrastructure and orchestration. Cool. But the best GTM agents aren't built by people who understand agents. They're built by people who understand the job the agent replaces.

That's why former SDRs, AEs, and RevOps people who learn to build will run circles around engineers who learn GTM secondhand.

The grind isn't the obstacle. It's the training data.
posts.cta: What's the manual work you did early in your career that now shapes how you build?

Shares a framework for aligning product, sales, and marketing through data-driven automation. Emphasizes the need for GTM engineers to bridge technical and revenue teams.

posts.title: Multi-agent systems are just pipelines
posts.hook: Everyone's building multi-agent systems. Most of them are just pipelines with extra steps.
posts.body:
Everyone's building multi-agent systems. Most of them are just pipelines with extra steps.

I run 50+ AI skills from a monorepo. Claude Code for orchestration, Sonnet for chat, Qwen 2.5 14B locally for high-freq crons, Opus for content generation. Four models, different jobs.

Here's what actually matters when you wire agents together:

1. Context handoffs. Every agent session writes a handoff doc so the next session picks up clean. No re-explaining. No context loss.
2. Routing logic. Not every task needs a team of agents. Most tasks need one focused agent with the right tools loaded. I built a routing skill that decides before spawning anything.
3. Failure boundaries. When an agent goes sideways mid-execution, it stops and re-plans. It doesn't keep pushing broken code hoping it works.

The Kubernetes-for-AI framing is accurate but misleading. Kubernetes solved orchestration for stateless containers. Agents are stateful, opinionated, and wrong 15% of the time. The hard part isn't scheduling them. It's knowing when to trust their output.

I've burned entire sessions watching agents confidently produce garbage because the routing was lazy. Now the rule is simple... one task per agent, verify before done, never mark complete without proof.
posts.cta: If you're building with multiple AI models, what's your routing logic? Or is everything going to one model?

posts.title: Outbound automation without intent is spam
posts.hook: Automated outbound without intent data is just spam at scale. I know because I built both versions.
posts.body:
Automated outbound without intent data is just spam at scale. I know because I built both versions.

Version 1: Pull a list from Apollo. Enrich with Clay. Throw it into a sequence. 2% reply rate. Most replies were "unsubscribe."

Version 2: Score accounts on hiring signals, tech stack changes, and funding events before they hit a sequence. Layer in website visitor data from PostHog. Only trigger outbound when the score crosses a threshold.

Same tools. Completely different results.

The problem with most outbound automation isn't the automation part. It's that people skip the intelligence layer. They go straight from "here's a list" to "here's a sequence" and wonder why deliverability tanks and nobody responds.

The intelligence layer is the boring part. It's building scoring rubrics in Clay that check 6 signals before a contact qualifies. It's writing Sculptor prompts that actually validate whether someone matches your ICP instead of just checking their title.

Automation multiplies whatever you feed it. Feed it garbage data and lazy targeting, you get garbage at scale. Feed it scored, intent-validated accounts, you get pipeline.

There's no shortcut past the scoring layer.
posts.cta: What signals are you scoring on before contacts hit your sequences?

posts.title: I run 4 websites from one repo
posts.hook: I run 4 live websites, a CRM integration, a Reddit scout, an SEO engine, and a daily content cron. All from one monorepo on a Mac Mini that sits on my desk.
posts.body:
I run 4 live websites, a CRM integration, a Reddit scout, an SEO engine, and a daily content cron. All from one monorepo on a Mac Mini that sits on my desk.

No DevOps team. No Terraform. No cloud bill anxiety.

The stack: Next.js for the sites, Cloudflare for hosting, Python scripts for the pipelines, launchd for scheduling, SQLite for state. The Mac Mini runs 24/7 as my always-on server. My MacBook is for building.

People overcomplicate infrastructure. I see founders spinning up Kubernetes clusters for apps that serve 200 users. I see teams with 4 engineers managing AWS bills bigger than their revenue.

Meanwhile my midnight cron generates SEO content, scores keywords, publishes blog posts, and syncs everything to GitHub. Total monthly cost for the compute... my electricity bill.

The lesson from shipping 5 products isn't about speed. It's about removing friction from the loop. When deploy means "git push" and your CI is a shell script, you ship more because shipping is easy.

I'm not anti-cloud. I'm anti-complexity-before-you-need-it. Start with the simplest thing that works. Scale when it breaks.
posts.cta: What's the most overengineered piece of your stack that could be replaced with a shell script?

inspired_by.author: Pieter Levels
inspired_by.summary: Pieter shared his latest product launch, a new AI-powered tool for remote workers, detailing the solo development process from idea to shipping in under a week. He emphasized the importance of building in public to gather feedback and iterate quickly. The post includes metrics on initial user signups and lessons learned from the MVP release.

posts.title: Content strategy for RevOps is backwards
posts.hook: Most RevOps content strategies start with "what should we write about?" That's backwards.
posts.body:
Most RevOps content strategies start with "what should we write about?" That's backwards.

Start with what you already know from the data.

Every RevOps team sits on a goldmine of content. Your CRM tells you which objections kill deals. Your enrichment data shows which ICPs convert. Your scoring rubrics encode years of pattern recognition.

Turn those into content and you're not writing thought leadership. You're publishing proof of work.

Here's what I do. My SEO engine pulls trending keywords daily, scores them against my ICP, and queues topics that match what I actually build. Then the content gets generated from real workflows, not hypothetical frameworks.

The post about Clay scoring rubrics? That's a real rubric running in production. The ABM walkthrough? That's my actual pipeline with names swapped out.

Storytelling in B2B isn't about being vulnerable or sharing your founder journey. It's about showing your work with enough specificity that someone could replicate it.

The bar for content isn't "is this interesting." It's "could someone use this tomorrow morning."

If your content strategy doesn't start from what your systems already know, you're just guessing at what to write.
posts.cta: What data does your team sit on that could be content but isn't?

posts.title: Plumber to programmer pipeline
posts.hook: I was a plumber before I was an SDR. Before that I was a line cook. The career pivot posts always skip the ugly middle.
posts.body:
I was a plumber before I was an SDR. Before that I was a line cook. The career pivot posts always skip the ugly middle.

The middle looks like this: you're 26, you have no degree, and you're learning Salesforce from YouTube videos at midnight after 10-hour days. Nobody's cheering. There's no "and then I raised a seed round" moment coming.

What actually happened: I got good at the boring repetitive work that nobody wanted to do. Data entry. List building. Manually researching accounts. That repetitive work taught me what needed to be automated before I knew what automation was.

Now I build GTM infrastructure. 50+ AI skills. A full ABM pipeline. 4 websites. Not because I had some grand vision. Because I kept solving the next problem in front of me and eventually the problems got more interesting.

The pivot wasn't one moment. It was 500 small decisions to keep learning instead of staying comfortable.

I think the reason builder backgrounds are underrated in tech is because we're trained to value credentials over calluses. But the person who's installed 200 water heaters understands systems thinking in a way that's hard to teach.

Repetition isn't the obstacle to creativity. It's the foundation.
posts.cta: What's your non-traditional background that actually makes you better at what you do now?

posts.title: Edge computing for GTM agents
posts.hook: Running AI models locally changed my GTM operation more than any SaaS tool this year.
posts.body:
Running AI models locally changed my GTM operation more than any SaaS tool this year.

I run Qwen 2.5 14B on Ollama for every high-frequency task that doesn't need frontier intelligence. Keyword scoring, content drafts, data validation, SEO classification. Stuff that runs 20+ times a day.

Cost per query: zero. Latency: sub-second. No rate limits. No API keys to rotate. No vendor deciding to 5x their pricing overnight.

For the tasks that need real reasoning, Opus handles content creation and strategic work. Sonnet runs the chat interface. Each model has a job based on what it's actually good at.

The edge computing conversation in AI is mostly about deploying inference closer to users. But for GTM ops, the win is deploying inference closer to your data. My enrichment scripts, scoring crons, and content pipelines all run on the same machine as the database. No round trips. No serialization overhead.

The serverless pitch is compelling until you're running 200 function invocations a day and your bill looks like you're training a model.

I'm not saying everyone should run local models. I'm saying the default of sending everything to an API endpoint deserves scrutiny. Especially when half your workload is classification tasks a 14B model handles fine.
posts.cta: Are you running any AI workloads locally, or is everything API-based?

posts.title: The scoring layer nobody builds
posts.hook: You don't have a pipeline problem. You have a scoring problem.
posts.body:
You don't have a pipeline problem. You have a scoring problem.

Most GTM teams I talk to run some version of this: pull accounts from Apollo or ZoomInfo, enrich in Clay, push to sequences. Then they wonder why conversion rates are flat.

The missing layer is scoring. Not lead scoring in your MAP that checks "opened email 3 times." Real account scoring that validates fit before a single touch happens.

Here's what my scoring layer checks before an account enters any sequence:

✅ Hiring signals (are they building the team that buys what I sell?)
✅ Tech stack match (verified through enrichment, not self-reported)
✅ Funding or growth signals within 6 months
✅ Website visitor behavior from PostHog
✅ Content engagement across channels
✅ Sculptor validation (does this company actually match the ICP when you dig in?)

Six signals. Each weighted. Threshold score before anything triggers.

The result: smaller lists, higher conversion, better deliverability because you're not burning your domain on accounts that were never going to buy.

Scaling outbound isn't about sending more emails. It's about being ruthlessly selective about who gets them. The scoring layer is the hardest part to build and the easiest part to skip. That's exactly why it's the differentiator.
posts.cta: How many signals do you validate before an account enters your outbound motion?

posts.title: Ship the ugly version
posts.hook: My first version of every tool I've built was embarrassing. That's the point.
posts.body:
My first version of every tool I've built was embarrassing. That's the point.

The Reddit scout started as a Python script that dumped raw JSON to a file. No UI. No filtering. Just a cron job and a prayer.

Now it scores opportunities, matches them to voice profiles, drafts responses, and queues them for approval in Slack. But it took 6 iterations to get there. Each one built on watching what actually mattered when I used the ugly version.

The builders who ship 5 products a year aren't better engineers. They just have a lower bar for v1.

Here's the pattern I've noticed... the people who ship slowly aren't stuck on hard problems. They're stuck on easy problems they've overcomplicated. They're adding auth before they have users. Building dashboards before they have data. Designing APIs before they know what the client needs.

My rule: if the first version takes more than a weekend, you're building the wrong first version.

The ugly version teaches you things the spec never will. It shows you which features actually matter, which edge cases are real, and which assumptions were wrong.

You can't architect your way to product-market fit. You have to ship your way there.
posts.cta: What's something you shipped ugly that turned into your best tool?

inspired_by.author: KP (Karthik Pattabiraman)
inspired_by.summary: KP documented shipping his 5th product in a year as a solo developer, a no-code automation tool, with transparent revenue numbers and build logs. He discussed challenges of working alone and how building in public helped attract early users and collaborators. Readers praised the authenticity and substance of his updates.

posts.title: AI agents need trust boundaries
posts.hook: I let an AI agent run unsupervised for 3 hours once. It confidently rewrote a working system into something that looked better and did less.
posts.body:
I let an AI agent run unsupervised for 3 hours once. It confidently rewrote a working system into something that looked better and did less.

That's when I built trust boundaries into everything.

The discourse around AI agent infrastructure treats reliability as a scaling problem. Build better orchestration, add more guardrails, implement retry logic. But the real problem is simpler... agents are confidently wrong just often enough to be dangerous.

My rules after getting burned:

1. Never mark a task complete without verification. The agent has to prove it works, not just say it works.
2. One task per agent. The moment you give an agent two objectives, it optimizes for the wrong one.
3. Failure stops execution. When something breaks mid-task, the agent re-plans instead of pushing through.
4. Human checkpoints at deploy boundaries. Agents can write code, run tests, build locally. They cannot push to production without sign-off.

This isn't anti-AI. I run my entire operation on AI agents. But I treat them like junior engineers, not senior architects. They're fast, capable, and need code review.

The teams that will win with agents aren't the ones with the most sophisticated orchestration. They're the ones with the clearest boundaries around what agents can and can't do autonomously.
posts.cta: Where do you draw the line between agent autonomy and human oversight in your workflows?

2. One task per agent. The moment you give an agent two objectives, it optimizes for the wrong one.
3. Failure stops execution. When something breaks mid-task, the agent re-plans instead of pushing through.
4. Human checkpoints at deploy boundaries. Agents can write code, run tests, build locally. They cannot push to production without sign-off.

This isn't anti-AI. high-performing operators run the entire operation on AI agents. But I treat them like junior engineers, not senior architects. They're fast, capable, and need code review.

The teams that will win with agents aren't the ones with the most sophisticated orchestration. They're the ones with the clearest boundaries around what agents can and can't do autonomously.
tones.provocateur:
I let an AI agent run unsupervised for 3 hours once. It confidently rewrote a working system into something that looked better and did less. And most people in this space won't admit it.

That's when I built trust boundaries into everything.

The discourse around AI agent infrastructure treats reliability as a scaling problem. Build better orchestration, add more guardrails, implement retry logic. But the real problem is simpler... agents are confidently wrong just often enough to be dangerous.
